text: The surgeon performs a right hemi-hepatectomy on a seventy-year-old patient with a large solitary metastasis in the right lobe. A reverse L-shaped skin incision is made starting from the xiphysternum, moving vertically towards the umbilicus, then transversely and subcostally to the right. Diathermy is used to divide adipose tissue and fascia, exposing the linea alba. The ligamentum teres and falciform ligament are divided and tied off. The Omnitract retractor is positioned for lateral retraction and lift, facilitating liver manipulation. The Pringle maneuver is applied with intermittent clamping after dividing the lesser sac and applying tape around the inflow.

The surgeon removes the gallbladder using a fundus-first approach, identifying and tying the cystic duct and artery. The common bile duct is visible during this process. A long tie is left on the cystic duct for traction during hilum dissection. The porta hepatis is dissected with a sweetheart retractor placed above it, and the assistant lifts the cystic duct tie while the surgeon uses a right angle to dissect vertically towards the portal vein. The peritoneum and lymphatics are divided, and any right accessory artery is identified.

The portal vein is visualized, and attention is directed to the right hepatic artery, located just above the right portal vein. The right hepatic artery is mobilized with a sling and divided with strong ties after confirming the presence of a good left hepatic artery. The portal vein bifurcation is identified using a platelet, and a right angle is placed between the right and left portal veins for vertical dissection. Chordate process vessels are mobilized and divided. A right angle and forceps are used to sling the right portal vein, with dissection directed posteriorly to avoid injury. The forceps lift the portal vein, revealing the tip of the right angle. The right portal vein is compressed to confirm the line of demarcation along Cantlie's line.

The surgeon divides the right portal vein using an endogia stapler, carefully maneuvering around the chordate process. The mobilization of the right liver lobe involves dividing peritoneal reflections along the coronary and right triangular ligaments, using fingers to push away soft tissues. The hepatocaval ligament is identified and mobilized with a right angle, which is hugged against the cava and pushed vertically until it emerges near the diaphragm, allowing safe division with a stapler.

The surgeon focuses on the right hepatic vein, dissecting superiorly between the right and middle hepatic veins using a right angle. The liver is flipped over to facilitate dissection towards the patient's left shoulder. Hydrostatic pressure is applied to identify the pathway, and a sling is passed around the vein. Space is created around the vein for the placement of the endogia stapler, which is positioned to avoid narrowing the vena cava.

The surgeon begins the liver transection along Cantlie's line, using a CUSA device with diathermy to divide the liver capsule. Retraction is applied to the left using a clip on the falciform ligament. The CUSA is used in a stroking technique to identify and expose vascular and biliary structures. A vein is visualized, and clips are applied around it using a rolling technique. The surgeon carefully burns small structures with the tip of the CUSA, avoiding deep holes. Bleeding on the cut surface is controlled with argon or by applying the Pringle maneuver intermittently.

The surgeon uses a stapler gun to divide the anterior and posterior pedicles, minimizing anatomical distortion and ensuring a reliable seal. The chordate process is visible and must be divided to access the posterior pedicle. Additional clips are applied if the pedicle is wide. After dividing both pedicles, the final transection involves identifying and tying veins at the back before removing the specimen.

After the specimen is removed, the surgeon uses diathermy or argon on the cut liver surface to achieve hemostasis, ensuring not to affect any ties or sutures. A swab is placed on the cut surface to check for bile leaks, which are repaired with sutures if found. A Robinson drain is typically used for drainage, and glue is applied to the cut surface. The falciform ligament is reconstructed to prevent torsion of the remnant liver.
The surgeon completes the mask closure using PDS sutures, followed by closing the skin with either staples or sutures, marking the end of the liver resection procedure.
